attributes.stem:
A 77-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of progressive confusion. He has a medical history of hypertension, for which he takes amlodipine. He does not take any diuretics. He has no other significant medical history and takes no other regular medications.

On examination, he appears mildly confused but is otherwise well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His mucous membranes are moist, and his jugular venous pressure is not elevated. There is no peripheral oedema, and his chest is clear on auscultation. Skin turgor is normal.

Investigations reveal:

  • Sodium: 122 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium: 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Urea: 5.2 mmol/L (2.5-7.8)

  • Creatinine: 78 µmol/L (60-110)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step to investigate the cause of this patient's hyponatraemia?
attributes.explanation:
This elderly patient presents with confusion and is found to have significant hyponatraemia (sodium 122 mmol/L) with normal renal function. The physical examination suggests a euvolemic state, which narrows down the differential diagnosis. In an elderly patient with euvolemic hyponatraemia, syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) is a key consideration.

Serum and urine osmolality is the most appropriate next step. These tests are crucial in differentiating the various causes of hyponatraemia, particularly in distinguishing SIADH from other causes. The osmolality testing allows stratification of hyponatraemia as either hypotonic or normotonic/hypertonic. In SIADH, we would expect to see low serum osmolality, inappropriately high urine osmolality (typically >100 mOsm/kg), and elevated urine sodium (typically >40 mmol/L).

Urine sodium is indeed an important test in the evaluation of hyponatraemia and would typically be measured alongside serum and urine osmolality. However, as a standalone test, it provides less comprehensive information than the combination of serum and urine osmolality.

Serum cortisol level is important to rule out adrenal insufficiency as a cause of hyponatraemia. While this should be part of the workup, it's not the most immediate next step given the clinical picture.

Thyroid function tests can be considered in the workup of hyponatraemia, as hypothyroidism can cause hyponatraemia. However, it's not the most urgent next step, and thyroid disorders typically cause milder degrees of hyponatraemia.

CT brain might be considered to investigate causes of confusion or to look for intracranial pathology that could cause SIADH (e.g., subarachnoid haemorrhage). However, given the gradual onset and the absence of focal neurological signs, it's not the most appropriate next step in investigating the hyponatraemia itself.

After confirming SIADH with osmolality and urine studies, further investigations would be directed at finding the underlying cause, which could include malignancy, medications, pulmonary disease, or central nervous system disorders.
learningPoints: Euvolemic hyponatraemia in the elderly should prompt investigation for syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH).
Measurement of serum and urine osmolality, as well as urine sodium, are key investigations in the workup of hyponatraemia.
Normal renal function does not exclude hyponatraemia as a cause of confusion in the elderly.
references: Adrogué HJ, Tucker BM, Madias NE. Diagnosis and management of hyponatremia: a review. Jama. 2022 Jul 19;328(3):280-91. Available online at ; https://emergencymed.org.il/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/jama_adrogu_2022_rv_220011_1657919726.49616.pdf
